sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: Cells were exposed to DNA-damaging radiation. Wild-type cells accumulated in G1, increased p21 expression, and repaired damage before re-entering the cell cycle. Cells lacking functional p53 failed to induce p21 and entered S phase with damaged DNA. A drug that mimicked p21 reduced S-phase entry in p53-deficient cells. The instructor emphasized that checkpoints prevent replication of damaged genomes.
questions.stem: What is the role of p53 after DNA damage?

choices.B: It promotes checkpoint arrest partly by inducing p21

explanations.B: Correct. p53 is a tumor suppressor transcription factor that can induce p21, inhibiting cyclin-CDK activity and promoting cell-cycle arrest.
explanations.A: Primase synthesizes RNA primers; p53 does not directly synthesize DNA primers.
explanations.C: p53 can regulate cyclin-CDK activity indirectly but does not instantly degrade all cyclins.
explanations.D: Ion channels are unrelated to the checkpoint described.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Cell-cycle distribution and p21 expression after DNA damage in wild-type and p53-deficient cells.

questions.stem: Why do p53-deficient cells enter S phase with damaged DNA?

choices.C: They fail to activate the G1 damage checkpoint
choices.D: They are permanently stuck in G0

explanations.C: Correct. Without functional p53, p21 induction and G1 arrest fail, allowing damaged DNA to be replicated.
explanations.A: The passage does not describe ATP failure.
explanations.B: They enter S phase, so DNA replication machinery is present.
explanations.D: They are not stuck in G0; they proceed into S phase.

questions.stem: What does the p21-mimic drug demonstrate?

choices.A: Restoring downstream CDK inhibition can partially rescue checkpoint arrest
choices.B: p21 is upstream of DNA damage
choices.C: p53 is unnecessary in all contexts

explanations.A: Correct. The drug reduces S-phase entry in p53-deficient cells, showing that a downstream checkpoint effector can compensate partly for loss of p53 signaling.
explanations.B: p21 acts downstream in the checkpoint response, not upstream of damage.
explanations.C: p53 still has many functions; the drug only partially rescues one output.
explanations.D: Radiation damage is the initiating condition.

questions.stem: Which condition has the highest S-phase entry after damage?

choices.B: p53 loss plus damage plus p21 mimic

explanations.D: Correct. Figure 1 shows p53-deficient damaged cells have high S-phase percentage compared with damaged wild type and p21-mimic treatment.
explanations.A: WT damaged cells arrest in G1 and have low S phase.
explanations.B: The p21 mimic lowers S-phase entry.
explanations.C: WT control is not the damaged checkpoint-failure condition.

questions.stem: Why is checkpoint failure associated with cancer risk?

choices.C: Damaged DNA can be replicated and mutations propagated to daughter cells
choices.A: It prevents all cell division forever
choices.B: It guarantees immediate apoptosis in every cell
choices.D: It converts RNA into protein without ribosomes

explanations.C: Correct. If cells replicate damaged DNA, mutations can be fixed into the genome and inherited by daughter cells, promoting genomic instability and cancer.
explanations.A: Checkpoint failure permits, not prevents, inappropriate division.
explanations.B: p53 loss can reduce apoptosis rather than guarantee it.
explanations.D: Translation is not the mechanism of cancer risk here.

questions.passage: A developmental biology lab compared normal cells with cells lacking Bax, a pro-apoptotic Bcl-2 family protein. After an intrinsic death signal, normal cells released cytochrome c from mitochondria, activated caspase-9 and caspase-3, and showed DNA fragmentation. Bax-deficient cells showed reduced cytochrome c release and much lower caspase activity. Adding a small molecule that directly activates caspase-3 increased DNA fragmentation even in Bax-deficient cells.
questions.stem: Where does cytochrome c release occur during intrinsic apoptosis?

choices.D: From the Golgi into extracellular fluid only

explanations.B: Correct. Intrinsic apoptosis involves mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization and release of cytochrome c into the cytosol, promoting apoptosome formation.
explanations.A: Ribosomes do not release cytochrome c.
explanations.C: Bile is unrelated to this pathway.
explanations.D: The key release is mitochondrial to cytosolic, not Golgi to outside only.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Cytochrome c release, caspase-3 activity, and DNA fragmentation under intrinsic apoptosis conditions.

questions.stem: Why does Bax deficiency reduce caspase activation?

choices.D: Bax promotes mitochondrial permeabilization upstream of cytochrome c release
choices.A: Bax is the final DNA-cutting enzyme

explanations.D: Correct. Bax helps trigger mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization. Without Bax, cytochrome c release and downstream caspase activation fall.
explanations.A: DNA fragmentation occurs downstream and is not directly performed by Bax.
explanations.B: The passage concerns pathway activation, not Bax transcription of caspase genes.
explanations.C: Bax is a Bcl-2 family apoptosis protein, not glycolytic enzyme.

questions.stem: What does direct caspase-3 activation show?

choices.A: Downstream caspase activation can bypass part of the mitochondrial defect
choices.C: Cytochrome c release becomes unnecessary for all apoptosis in vivo
choices.D: DNA fragmentation occurs before caspase activation

explanations.A: Correct. Activating caspase-3 restores DNA fragmentation despite low cytochrome c release, showing that downstream executioner caspase activity can bypass the upstream Bax-dependent step.
explanations.B: The pathway places Bax upstream of caspase activation.
explanations.C: The experiment shows bypass in this setup, not that cytochrome c is irrelevant in all apoptosis.
explanations.D: Caspase-3 activity leads to fragmentation rather than following it.

questions.stem: Which condition has low cytochrome c release but high DNA fragmentation?

choices.C: Bax deficient plus death signal only

explanations.B: Correct. The caspase-3 activator condition keeps cytochrome c release low but raises DNA fragmentation, matching the described bypass.
explanations.A: Normal cells have high cytochrome c and high fragmentation.
explanations.C: Bax-deficient cells with signal only have low values for both downstream outputs.
explanations.D: Untreated normal cells are not shown as the high-fragmentation bypass condition.

questions.stem: How does apoptosis differ from necrosis?

choices.C: Apoptosis is a regulated cell-death program involving caspases
choices.A: Apoptosis always causes membrane rupture first

explanations.C: Correct. Apoptosis is controlled and commonly caspase-mediated, whereas necrosis is more associated with uncontrolled injury and inflammation.
explanations.A: Early membrane rupture is more characteristic of necrotic injury.
explanations.B: Caspase-9 is part of intrinsic apoptosis, not necrosis.
explanations.D: Mitosis is cell division, not programmed cell death.
